channel.title: Home on Bioinformatics, R, Python, etc.
channel.description: Recent content in Home on Bioinformatics, R, Python, etc.

item.title: Benchmarking String Joining Functions in R
item.description: I recently had to concatenate tens of millions of strings into a single column of strings (thanks Bismark coverage files…) to be used as a unique rownames for a combined data.frame of methylation values for multiple samples. I was surprised when even my data.table code which was something like, dt[, Location := paste(chr, start, sep = "-")], was taking minutes. So then, what is the fastest way to combine a bunch of strings?

item.title: Base R Tips & Tricks
item.description: I think the tidyverse is great and I love data.table even more but as I’ve started to develop my own R packages I’ve really tried to cut down on dependencies as much as possible. Understanding base R has also proven to be very useful when it comes to manipulating data and objects from Bioconductor packages. Here, I hope to write down some useful base R (and maybe a some data.

item.title: Estimating K-mer Probabilities
item.description: This was a challenge problem set forth in Bioinformatics Algorithms An Active Learning Approach by Phillip Compeau and Pavel Pevzner (2018). I borrow heavily from their examples. The Problem What is the probability that a some k-mer will appear at least \(t\) times as a sub-string of a random string of length \(N\) composed of alphabet \(A\)? For example, what is the probability that some 9-mer appears 3 or more times in a random DNA string of length 500?

I am a Bioinformatics Scientist at the Coriell Institute. I have been learning R, Python, Bioinformatics, and all of the things that go along with those subjects for about 3 years now. I’m always trying to get better at mathematics and statistics as well as trying to pick up new programming languages like C. Assorted Links Github LinkedIn My R Package
